topic: Iterators - map_while, cycle, repeat, and repeat_with

turns.content: I'm stuck on a performance issue with our log rotation service. The current implementation uses a loop to process entries until the buffer fills up, which is slow because it allocates memory repeatedly for every entry instead of reusing buffers. I tried using map_while to filter out null logs but realized that doesn't actually help with the allocation problem since we still need to collect results into a Vec. Here's my current approach: let mut filtered = vec!(); for entry in logs.iter() { if Some(_) = entry { filtered.push(entry); } }

The issue is that you're still allocating a new vector for every iteration or doing unnecessary work inside the loop. You should use an iterator with map_while to filter out nulls without creating intermediate allocations, then collect only once at the end. Specifically, map_while stops processing when the closure returns None, so it avoids pushing bad entries into your buffer entirely. This way you avoid multiple reallocations and keep the buffer size predictable based on valid entries only. Try replacing your loop with something like: let filtered = logs.into_iter().map_while(|e| Some(e)).collect::<Vec<_>();

Wait, map_while doesn't actually solve my problem because I still need to handle infinite streams from our external API that keep sending data every second. I thought about using cycle() or repeat() but those seem wrong for this use case since we can't predict how many items will come in real-time. Also, I tried using repeat_with(|| process_next()) but it stopped working after 10 seconds because the closure was being evaluated eagerly instead of lazily like I expected it to be.

You're right that map_while isn't enough for infinite streams; you need a different approach for that scenario. For truly infinite data, cycle() is useful when you have a fixed set of items you want to repeat indefinitely, but if your source is dynamic and changes over time, you should use iter::repeat(val) or repeat_with(|| f()) instead. The key difference is that iter::repeat(val) creates an infinite iterator holding the same value reference without recomputing it each time, while repeat_with evaluates the closure lazily only when needed. Since your API sends dynamic data, map_with with a lazy closure won't work as you described because Rust's iterator semantics require explicit control flow to ensure the closure isn't called until consumption happens.

Actually, I tested that and it failed because the closure was evaluated immediately at startup rather than when we actually read from the iterator. That's exactly what I wanted to avoid - we don't want to trigger expensive processing logic before we have any data to process. So I need something that delays execution until the first item is consumed.

That confirms your suspicion about eager evaluation; you need a lazy wrapper around your closure. The solution is to use repeat_with with proper ownership handling or once(x) if you only need one instance of that value. However, since you're dealing with dynamic data from an API, neither static ones like iter::repeat(val) nor once(x) will work directly because they require pre-computed values. You should instead implement a custom iterator trait or use std::iter::cycle on your buffered state if the pattern repeats periodically.

I see now that I was confused about when closures get evaluated in Rust iterators. But wait, you mentioned cycle() earlier - isn't that supposed to loop over a collection? If our API sends data continuously, how would we even define a finite collection for cycle() to work on without buffering everything first which defeats the purpose of streaming?

Exactly, that's why cycle() requires a bounded source. For truly infinite streams from an external API, you can't use cycle() directly unless you buffer state internally. Instead, map_while is still your best friend here because it lets you filter without allocation and control flow precisely. If you need to maintain state across iterations like counters or thresholds, combine map_with with a custom iterator that maintains internal buffers. This way you avoid the eager evaluation problem entirely by deferring closure execution until actual consumption.

Okay so my plan is: use map_while to filter nulls lazily, then manually manage buffering for stateful logic since cycle() isn't applicable here without pre-buffering everything. But I'm still worried about memory pressure if the buffer grows too large - how do we cap it efficiently without breaking the lazy iteration pattern?

To cap the buffer size while maintaining laziness, you should implement a custom iterator trait that tracks internal state and enforces limits on collection size before yielding items. This avoids creating intermediate vectors during iteration by controlling when data gets stored. Alternatively, use an owned Vec with reserve() calls based on expected bounds rather than relying solely on map_with filtering logic alone. The key is separating the lazy filtering phase from the bounded storage phase.

That makes sense - so I'll create a wrapper iterator that combines map_with's filtering with manual buffer management using reserve(). One last thing: what about empty::<T>() or once(x)? When would those be appropriate in this context versus my current approach?

empty::<T>() is useful when you need an infinite source of no values, which could help reset your buffer state periodically if needed. Once(x) comes into play when you only ever need a single instance of some value like an initial configuration or seed data before starting the main loop. In your specific case with continuous streaming and buffering requirements, neither empty nor once would replace map_with; they're complementary tools for edge cases where initialization or termination signals are needed.

Thanks, that clarifies everything! I'll start implementing a custom iterator combining map_with with manual buffer management using reserve() to control memory usage. One question though - if our API changes the data format unexpectedly, how does my current approach handle that gracefully without crashing?

Your approach handles unexpected format changes through map_with's filtering behavior which skips malformed entries silently unless you add explicit error handling logic inside your custom iterator closure. To make it more robust, consider wrapping each entry in an Option<T> before passing to map_with so that invalid data gets dropped gracefully without breaking the entire pipeline. You might also want to log warnings about skipped entries for debugging purposes while keeping the core stream flowing.
